The company is investing heavily in AI and it is a very diversified business that might profit in several ways from the AI-revolution. And while Alibaba might be able to grow at a high pace (high single digits or even double digits) for several years, the stock is still trading for a more reasonable valuation multiple than several U.S. AI companies.

Of course, this is not the best argument to make, but we should not ignore that Alibaba is still trading about 50% below its all-time high – indicating at least that sentiment was once a lot more bullish. And I see the previous all-time high certainly as a reasonable target within the next one to three years as Alibaba should be able to grow the stock price at a high pace and certainly outperform the market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EWZS: Brazilian Small-Caps Still Look Appealing Despite New Macro Challenges
data.description:
Opportunity Overview

Brazil's economy faces weaker growth and other economic risks this year, yet small-cap equities appear attractively priced in the market. While issues like inflation and rising debt levels have impacted Brazil, it has emerged victorious in other high-risk areas, such as the trade war tensions of 2025.

Brazilian equities have been one of the main drivers of emerging market returns in 2025.

Brazil still looks like an excellent hold amid the economic setbacks ahead in 2026 and 2027. The country's Central Bank has arguably been overcautious with rates, relative to Latam peers, and it will be in a better position to cut rates to support growth in 2026. Inflation has been tame, and the economy is poised to benefit from any increase in certain commodity prices, such as oil, iron, or agricultural products, due to its export structure.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (EWZS) is a solid, attractively priced investment in the market, even after its stellar run in 2025. Investors should hold this position and accumulate during any potential dips in 2026.

Brazil's Economic Outlook

Brazil's economy has improved massively since 2022, most notably in combating inflation, and one of the main challenges it faces now is slower growth leading into 2026.

Weak Growth in Recent Quarters: Brazil's Finance Ministry recently lowered its growth forecasts for 2025 to 2.2% following weaker performance in Q2 2025. This growth was at the weakest levels experienced in the past five years, and this trend may continue in subsequent quarters.

Brazil's GDP Annual Growth Rate

Growth Will Decline Next Year: Santander Research projects that Brazil's GDP growth will fall below 2% in 2026 and 2027, which will be well below its post-COVID average. Other measures, like its new VAT tax reform, could help support growth. However, the economy will also need other supporting factors, such as trade stability and lower interest rates, to bring growth back to its historical average.

Brazil Has Been Cautious With Interest Rates: Although inflation has improved substantially, the country's Central Bank has taken a cautious approach to its monetary policy, which will result in slower growth. Brazil's Central Bank chose to keep its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 15% last November.

Brazil's Benchmark Interest Rate

This move is extremely cautious relative to some of its regional peers, which have kept rates much lower despite similar inflationary risks.

Country May 2024 Benchmark Interest Rate Current Benchmark Interest Rate
Brazil 10.5% 15%
Colombia 11.75% 9.25%
Chile 6% 4.75%
Peru 5.75% 4.25%
Mexico 11% 7.25%

Inflation Improvements: The improvement in inflation has been broad-based, as energy prices have dropped, and grocery prices only rose by 4.5% YoY in October. Inflation should remain moderate in 2026, slightly above its previous pre-Covid lows.

Diversified Commodities Growth: Brazil is poised to benefit from any positive commodity price movements, particularly in areas like oil, soybeans, and iron, which account for 5.1% of GDP but 14.3% of its exports.

Soaring Sovereign Debt: One common issue that has affected all emerging markets, including regional peers, has been the increase in sovereign debt in emerging markets. Brazil has improved substantially since 2020, but still has higher debt levels relative to peers like Chile.

Trade War Risks: Brazil is also vulnerable to trade war tensions, as the United States is a major trading partner, yet its relationship with China has been very fruitful, allowing it to offset weaker performance in the US market.

Brazil's exports rose by nearly 2% during the first ten months of this year, despite the 30+% plunge in exports to the United States.

Unique Small Cap Approach

Small-cap stocks in Brazil appear very attractively positioned at the moment, but may be vulnerable to short-term pullbacks in the coming quarters due to weaker macro data from Brazil and a general correction in emerging market equities.

Small Caps Have Underperformed: Brazilian small-cap stocks performed exceptionally well in 2025, following decades of massive underperformance. Other EM small-cap stocks have outperformed Brazil since 2011.

Appeals of this ETF: The iShares MSCI Brazil Small Cap ETF is an ideal vehicle to maintain exposure to Brazil, mainly because of its low valuation. The ETF currently trades at 9.5x earnings and 1.06x book value. The ETF also has an attractive composition relative to other Latam ETFs, as it is overweight consumers and underweight financials, a unique trait among emerging market ETFs.

Other ETFs: If you want to gain exposure to Brazil, some of your main options include other general Brazil-focused ETFs, which primarily focus on mid-caps and large caps. While this ETF outperformed its peers in 2025, it has been a long-term underperformer, historically more vulnerable to macroeconomic setbacks in Brazil.

Brazil's Relative Performance: Brazilian equities have outperformed peers like Chile and Colombia, and have been outperformed by Peru and Mexico. While Brazilian equities have been in the middle in terms of performance in the past decade, small-cap stocks have been massive underperformers, underperforming the peer average by over 60 percentage points.

The outperformance of EM equities in 2025 is a new trend, which may not continue under weakening economic conditions in 2026.

Risks and Final Thoughts

As economic risks in Brazil increase, it may be more ideal to hold onto larger ETFs that focus on mid-cap and large-cap stocks instead. These ETFs have outperformed strongly since May 2022, when inflation began to pick up globally.

Brazil's economy will still face a lot of risks in 2026. Its growth will likely be slower, despite VAT reforms and its strong positioning to cut interest rates, which could cause equity markets to sell off in 2026. The country's public debt levels are also above those in other regional markets.

Emerging markets have also had a very stellar run and could be due for a pullback on the back of any macro or geopolitical risks. While markets like Brazil have been able to lean on other major trade partners like China in 2025, trade war shocks could have a spillover effect on economic and equity market performance in 2026. Moreover, the US and Brazil have been having open dialogues recently and have reduced tariffs in some areas.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small Cap ETF is an absolute gem based on valuation, and definitely looks like a safe hold at the moment. However, other larger ETFs like the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF could be safer bets, as they have historically shown their ability to have more stable performance amid economic shocks.
